metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct AC3's CI-gate rationale: confirm that modifying plan_review_T5c.md body text does NOT affect the derived prompt index (index.json is review-category-only), and replace the 'prompt-index drift check' reference with the actual relevant gate (the criteria_routing.json parity gate via `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing`, and any criteria-guide regeneration if T5c's routing checklist text changes).
evidence: plan_review_T5c.md front-matter: 'category: plan-review-criterion' (line 8)
src/rebar/llm/prompting/prompt_library.py line 58: CRITERION_CATEGORY = 'plan-review-criterion' (separate from 'review')
src/rebar/llm/prompting/prompt_library.py lines 13-23: index.json is derived from packaged reviewers with category=='review' only; plan-review-criterion files are excluded
tests/unit/test_prompt_index.py line 59-64: test_index_on_disk_matches_regenerated checks src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json, which does not include plan-review-criterion prompts
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py line 429-452: the REAL relevant gate for criteria changes is validate_packaged_routing (criteria_routing.json parity gate)
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'the derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it' and references a 'prompt-index drift check' as the relevant CI gate. However, plan_review_T5c.md has front-matter category: plan-review-criterion, and the derived prompt index (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json) is built exclusively from files with category: review (confirmed in prompts.py and prompt_library.py). Editing only the body of T5c.md will never require or affect index.json regeneration. The true relevant CI gate for this change is the criteria_routing.json parity gate (validate_packaged_routing), which checks routing consistency — not the prompt-index drift gate. The plan's regeneration premise is either vacuously true (a no-op) or points at the wrong artifact, which could mislead the implementer.
findings.location: AC3: 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes.'
scenarios: An implementer runs 'python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts regenerate-index' expecting to satisfy AC3 — the index.json is unchanged, giving false confidence that the gate is passing for the right reason.
The actual CI gate that protects prompt/routing coherence (validate_packaged_routing) is not mentioned in AC3, leaving it ambiguous whether that gate was considered.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3's oracle text with: 'Oracle: `make lint && make test` green locally; the `criteria_routing.json` parity gate (`python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing`) passes. Note: `plan_review_T5c.md` is a `plan-review-criterion` file and does NOT appear in the reviewer `index.json`; no prompt-index regeneration is required for a body-only edit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3's CI oracle: replace the reference to the 'prompt-index drift check' with the criteria-routing parity gate (`python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing` / `make lint && make test` where the parity gate is the relevant step), since plan_review_T5c.md has `category: plan-review-criterion` and is not indexed by the prompt-index mechanism.
evidence: plan_review_T5c.md front-matter: 'category: plan-review-criterion'

src/rebar/llm/prompting/prompts.py build_prompt_index: 'if not prompt.is_reviewer: continue  # only reviewers populate the selection catalog' and is_reviewer property: 'return self.category == "review"'

.github/workflows/test.yml: prompt-index drift gate diffs only src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json; the separate 'Criteria-routing parity gate' runs python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing
ABSENCE: no path connects plan_review_T5c.md edits to reviewers/index.json regeneration
findings.finding: AC3 names 'the prompt-index drift check' as the CI oracle for the T5c prompt change, but plan_review_T5c.md has category: plan-review-criterion and is excluded from reviewers/index.json (build_prompt_index only indexes category == 'review' prompts). Editing this file will not cause the prompt-index drift gate to fire; the gate that actually validates criteria_routing.json consistency is the separate criteria-routing parity gate (python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing). AC3's named oracle is the wrong gate and will pass vacuously, giving false confidence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3: 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes.'
scenarios: Implementer runs make lint && make test, the prompt-index drift gate passes vacuously (T5c is not in scope for it), and the implementer gets false confidence that the right CI gate validated their change.
A reviewer checking AC3's oracle never sees it red, even with a broken change, because the named gate is wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 with: 'The CI criteria-routing parity gate passes (python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing green); the prompt-index drift gate is not relevant since plan_review_T5c.md has category: plan-review-criterion and is excluded from reviewers/index.json.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3's CI oracle: replace the prompt-index drift gate (which only covers `category: review` files) with the actual verification that applies to `plan-review-criterion` prompts — e.g. a `make lint` front-matter schema parse, a grep-based oracle, or an explicit statement that no derived index regeneration is needed for this category.
evidence: plan_review_T5c.md front-matter: `category: plan-review-criterion` (not `category: review`)
prompts.py build_prompt_index(): `if not prompt.is_reviewer: continue  # only reviewers populate the selection catalog`
is_reviewer property: `return self.category == 'review'`
CI drift gate: `git diff --exit-code -- src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` — diffs only reviewer-category-derived artifacts
ABSENCE: no fallback verification path stated for the case where the drift gate does not apply to the changed file
findings.finding: AC3 names 'the prompt-index drift check (part of the test suite)' as the CI oracle for this change, but plan_review_T5c.md has category: plan-review-criterion — not category: review. The build_prompt_index() function explicitly skips any prompt where is_reviewer is False (i.e. category != 'review'), so editing this file produces zero delta in reviewers/index.json. The cited CI protocol cannot exercise the changed artifact, and no alternative verification path is specified for this case.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes.'
scenarios: An executor edits plan_review_T5c.md, runs `make lint && make test`, the prompt-index drift gate passes trivially (no diff), and concludes AC3 is satisfied — while the actual structural validity of the edited prompt file goes unverified by the named gate.
AC3's conditional 'if the build requires it' is never true for a plan-review-criterion file, making the entire AC3 a no-op oracle that passes vacuously.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3's oracle clause with one that actually exercises plan-review-criterion files. Since plan_review_T5c.md is not a reviewer-catalog entry, the drift gate is irrelevant; the correct oracle is either: (a) the existing `make lint && make test` alone (drop the drift-gate claim), OR (b) a specific front-matter parse/lint step that validates plan-review-criterion files. Update AC3 to read: 'Oracle: `make lint && make test` green locally; no prompt-index regeneration is needed since plan_review_T5c.md has category: plan-review-criterion and does not populate reviewers/index.json.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `grep -ni "does not touch\|in-plan" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -ni "one finding per" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The same-round dedup rule introduces '(surface, defect) pair' as the dedup key but neither 'surface' nor 'defect' is defined as an enum or given a value vocabulary anywhere in the plan or in the existing plan_review_T5c.md. The T5c agent implementing this rule cannot determine whether two findings share the same pair — is 'surface' an endpoint name, a credential path, a T5c sub-check key (e.g. secret_lifecycle), or a STRIDE category? Is 'defect' a free-form label or one of the T5c checklist keys? Without this vocabulary the rule is unenforceable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 ('one finding per (surface, defect) pair') / src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md (proposed addition)
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the dedup rule as: 'One finding per (named asset/component, T5c sub-check key) pair — where the asset/component is the specific resource the plan names (e.g. an SSM parameter, a credential, an endpoint) and the sub-check key is one of: trust_boundary | access_classification | data_protection | least_privilege | secret_lifecycle. Additional angles on the same (asset, sub-check) pair are folded into one finding's text.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'derived prompt index' in the plan body (e.g. which file/artifact it is and what command regenerates it) so AC3 is unambiguous to implement.
evidence: AC3 introduces 'derived prompt index' without explanation
The plan body (Evidence section) never mentions a prompt index or index-regeneration step
The Files section says '(+ regenerated prompt index if applicable)' — parenthetical, not elaborated
The oracle for AC3 is only `make lint && make test`, which does not verify the index artifact directly
findings.finding: The term 'derived prompt index' (used in AC3) is introduced in an acceptance criterion without any definition or description in the plan body or evidence section, making it ambiguous to an implementer who must determine what artifact to regenerate and when the 'build requires it'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it…'
scenarios: An implementer edits plan_review_T5c.md, runs make lint && make test, they pass, but the implementer doesn't know whether they also needed to run a separate index-regeneration step — the criterion is unverifiable without knowing what 'derived prompt index' refers to.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the plan body, e.g.: 'The derived prompt index (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json or equivalent — regenerated via `make prompts`) must be updated after editing plan_review_T5c.md; the CI prompt-drift gate in the test suite will catch staleness.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a behavioral validation step: define a small golden-set corpus (e.g., 3–5 representative plans — at least one with a genuine in-scope secret, one with only out-of-scope adjacent assets, one with near-duplicate findings) and include a CI-runnable fixture or manual pilot that runs the updated T5c prompt against this corpus and asserts expected finding presence/absence, so recall and precision can be confirmed before merge.
evidence: AC oracles are: grep for text presence, `make lint && make test` (prompt-index drift check) — none exercise T5c behavioral output
Plan's own motivation: '288 advisory findings in the last 7 days with zero blocks ever' — a behavioral regression baseline already exists implicitly
ABSENCE: no golden-set fixture, A/B test, pilot run, or before/after comparison of T5c outputs on a known plan corpus is specified
findings.finding: The plan's acceptance criteria only verify that the new scope and dedup rule text is present in the prompt (grep/structural oracles) and that CI lint/tests pass, but include no behavioral validation that the prompt change actually reduces out-of-scope false positives without harming recall on genuine in-scope security gaps. Prompt engineering changes to LLM behavior are inherently uncertain: the same wording that suppresses false positives may also suppress true positives, and this cannot be established by reasoning alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullet points) / Files: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md
scenarios: The new in-plan-asset scope rule is worded too broadly and suppresses a genuine finding about a credential the plan does transmit — regression goes undetected because no behavioral test exists.
The dedup rule conflates two distinct (surface, defect) pairs into one finding, hiding a real gap — again undetected without a behavioral oracle.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a `tests/fixtures/t5c_golden/` directory with 2–3 minimal plan stubs and a pytest parametrize test that calls the T5c reviewer and asserts: (a) a plan with a genuinely in-scope credential still produces a finding, (b) a plan that only references an adjacent unmodified credential produces zero T5c findings, (c) a plan with two near-duplicate (surface, defect) pairs produces exactly one finding. Add this test to the CI gate referenced in AC bullet 3.

findings.finding: The proving commands for AC1 and AC2 verify only that specific text strings were inserted into the prompt file (structural grep checks), but no acceptance criterion exercises the modified T5c prompt behaviorally against the known failure cases cited in the evidence (e.g., the Gerrit-password out-of-scope finding, the temp-key-file near-duplicate pair). The plan's stated end-state is that T5c 'stops claiming out-of-scope assets and stops emitting near-duplicates while preserving recall on genuine gaps' — but the union of steps (add text → grep confirms → make test green) proves only that the text was added, not that the added rules produce the desired behavioral change. A prompt change that syntactically satisfies the grep oracle could still leave the LLM ignoring the new rules.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2; plan Evidence section (failure shape examples)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A prompt-evaluation fixture (e.g., tests/reviewers/t5c_scope_dedup_test.py or equivalent) replays (a) the Gerrit-password out-of-scope scenario and asserts zero findings, (b) the ticket-9e76 near-duplicate scenario and asserts exactly one finding, and (c) a genuine in-scope secret-exposure scenario and asserts at least one finding. Oracle: `make test` runs and all three assertions pass.'

findings.finding: The plan's stated constraint 'without changing...recall on genuinely in-scope security gaps' has no end-state-observable proof. All four ACs verify text presence or routing stability; none verify that legitimate in-scope security findings are not inadvertently suppressed by the new scope rule. If the wording of the in-plan-asset rule is overly broad, recall could silently degrade with no AC catching it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four ACs; plan goal statement ('without...changing...recall on genuinely in-scope security gaps')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Given a fixture plan that explicitly creates and transmits an API key, T5c still emits at least one advisory finding after the scope rule is applied. Oracle: the existing prompt-test harness (or a new fixture) exercises this case and passes under make test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale explaining why prompt-instruction addition is the right mechanism for both rules (e.g., it keeps the change to a single file, preserves routing/posture unchanged per AC4, and is codebase-aligned with how existing T5c behavior is already controlled).
evidence: The plan's 'Evidence' section explains the problem and references ADR 0018 context ('ADR 0018's trust-boundary scope gate already bounds WHEN T5c fires; this task bounds WHAT it may claim once fired') but never affirmatively states why prompt-instruction is the right mechanism.
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'We chose to modify the prompt rather than X because Y' appears anywhere in the plan.
G6 rubric requires: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding).'
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing prompt-instruction as the mechanism (versus, e.g., post-processing dedup or schema-enforcement), leaving the 'why this approach fits' question unanswered.
findings.location: Plan body / Evidence + Acceptance Criteria sections (no rationale section present)
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer reading the plan cannot determine whether post-processing dedup was considered and rejected, or whether it was simply not considered — the rationale is absent.
A future author hardening another rubric prompt has no guidance on why prompt-instruction was preferred over a structural enforcement approach.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan body (e.g., after the Evidence paragraph): 'Approach: prompt-instruction addition to plan_review_T5c.md is the right mechanism because (a) it is a single-file change with minimal blast radius, (b) it preserves the routing and posture contract unchanged (AC4), and (c) the existing prompt already governs T5c behavior entirely through instructions — adding two rules stays within the same pattern without requiring new code or schema changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether a pipeline-level (post-processing) dedup mechanism already exists or is planned; if not, acknowledge in the plan that prompt-instruction dedup is the chosen approach with the known tradeoff that LLM compliance is probabilistic (not guaranteed), so the AC's oracle only confirms the rule is present, not that it is effective.
evidence: The plan's Evidence section names the observed failure ('same-round near-duplicates, ticket 9e76: two findings both demanding temp-key-file deletion') but proposes only a prompt-instruction fix, with no discussion of whether a downstream dedup/consolidation layer exists or is needed.

No existing `one finding per` rule was found in any plan_review_T*.md file (confirmed by codebase search), so the approach is not redundant — but this also means there is no prior architectural solution being extended, making the sufficiency assumption unverified.
LLM prompt-instruction compliance for dedup is probabilistic; if a hard guarantee is needed, a post-processing layer would be the established approach.
findings.finding: The plan implicitly asserts that a prompt-level same-round dedup instruction is *sufficient* to eliminate near-duplicate findings, without checking whether a pipeline-level (post-processing) dedup mechanism already exists or whether the absence of one would leave probabilistic prompt compliance as the sole guard.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'one finding per (surface, defect) pair; additional criteria angles on the same pair are folded into that finding's text, not emitted separately'
scenarios: The prompt-level dedup instruction is followed inconsistently by the LLM across runs, leaving near-duplicates in production while the AC's grep oracle ('one finding per') only verifies the text is present in the prompt, not its effectiveness.
A post-processing dedup layer already exists (or is introduced by a sibling task) and the prompt-level rule is redundant — no architectural accounting for this interaction.

findings.finding: AC3 refers to 're-generating the derived prompt index' and 'the prompt-index drift check', but plan_review_T5c.md has category: plan-review-criterion (not category: review), so it never contributes to reviewers/index.json or its regeneration. The actual derived artifact governed by the CI drift gate for plan-review criteria is docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md, regenerated via `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide` and validated by validate_criteria_guide (folded into the validate-routing gate). This artifact is entirely unmentioned in the plan, leaving an implementer likely to target the wrong regeneration path or skip regeneration altogether.
findings.location: AC3 — 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 with: 'Run `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry regenerate-criteria-guide` to refresh `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md`; the CI parity gate (`python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing`) must pass green. Oracle: `make lint && make test` green, with the validate-routing gate showing OK.'

findings.finding: The in-plan-asset scope rule specifies that a finding must name 'the plan section/step that creates, modifies, or transmits the asset at issue,' but provides no co-located protocol for how the T5c agent enumerates which assets are 'touched' by the plan. The decision rule — what verbs or operations count as 'touching' an asset, and how to scan plan steps to build that set — is referenced implicitly but never specified.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
